# Input and Output Operations

1. Write a programme that asks the user to enter a single integer, then displays it on the screen.
2. Create a programme that reads two floating-point numbers from the user and prints their difference.
3. Design an application that asks the user for their year of birth, then prints the previous year (one less).
4. Write a programme that reads three integers from the user and prints their average as a floating-point value.
5. Create a programme that asks the user to enter two letters (characters), then prints them in reverse order.
6. Write a programme that asks the user to enter a single floating-point number, then doubles its value and prints the result.
7. Create a programme that reads a floating-point number from the user representing an amount in dollars, then prints how much that is in euros, using a fixed exchange rate (e.g. 1 dollar = 0.85 euros).
8. Write a programme that displays the following text on the screen: `This is a quote: "I often use the C language."`. Ensure that the quotation marks are correctly displayed as part of the string.
9. Create a programme that prints a Windows folder path to standard output, e.g. `C:\Program Files\MyApplication\` (hardcoded, without retrieving anything from the system) or `C:\\Program Files\\MyApplication\\`.
10. Design a programme that demonstrates how to print the following text on the screen: `Special characters: \t (tab), \n (newline), % (percent), \\ (backslash).`
11. Write a programme that reads two rational numbers from standard input representing the lengths of the legs of a right-angled triangle. Then calculate and display the length of the hypotenuse.
12. Write a programme that reads an integer from standard input and prints its absolute value to standard output.
13. Write a programme that reads a floating-point number from standard input and prints its absolute value to standard output.
14. Find an example and display on standard output a case where the addition of floating-point numbers is not associative.
